title: Abolitionists Must Stand Firm to Oppose Murder, Love Murderers -
author: Guest Writer
orientation: A short opinion piece by abolitionist Aaron Holland arguing that women who voluntarily seek abortions should be held legally culpable for murder alongside abortionists, framing punishment under equal-protection law as an act of love that can lead to repentance. Aimed at pro-life Christians who currently exempt mothers from punishment.
audiences: Pro-life Christians who oppose punishing women who abort
Abolitionists building the case for equal protection laws
Pastors and discipleship leaders thinking about sin, forgiveness, and consequences
Legislators and policy advocates drafting abortion-criminalization bills
Skeptics who see abolitionism as unloving or harsh

questions.question: Why do most pro-life advocates refuse to punish women who get abortions?
questions.audience: Pro-life Christians who oppose punishing women who abort
questions.answer: Holland says the typical pro-life position singles out abortionists as the killers and treats mothers as victims who deserve only grace. He concedes the motive is usually genuine compassion and a desire to extend forgiveness, but he argues that compassion is being mistakenly applied.
questions.quote: Usually, this argument is made from a genuinely compassionate perspective. Pro-life people want to emphasize the evil of abortionists without addressing the guilt of the mother. Perhaps they want to give grace and emphasize that there is forgiveness for this sin.

questions.question: Does Christian forgiveness mean a mother who aborts should escape legal consequences?
questions.audience: Pastors and discipleship leaders thinking about sin, forgiveness, and consequences
questions.answer: Holland affirms that forgiveness for abortion is genuinely available in Christ for both mothers and abortionists. But he draws a sharp line between spiritual forgiveness and exemption from civil punishment, insisting the two are not the same thing.
questions.quote: There most certainly is forgiveness for mothers (and abortionists) who have committed the sin of abortion — and that forgiveness is found in Jesus Christ alone. But forgiveness does not mean living without consequences.

questions.question: Is a mother who chooses abortion as guilty as the abortionist?
questions.audience: Abolitionists building the case for equal protection laws
questions.answer: In cases where the mother is not coerced, Holland says she is the one who initiates the killing by seeking out the procedure, which makes her morally on par with the abortionist. He acknowledges coerced abortions are a separate category his argument does not address.
questions.quote: In the most common circumstances, wherein a mother purposefully seeks to have an abortion, the mother and the abortionist are both equally guilty of murdering a preborn child.

questions.question: What scriptural basis does the article give for punishing murder?
questions.audience: Pastors and discipleship leaders thinking about sin, forgiveness, and consequences
questions.answer: Holland reaches back to Genesis, citing Cain's exile for killing Abel and the post-flood command in Genesis 9:6 that bloodshed be answered with bloodshed because humans bear God's image. He treats consequences for murder as both a universal human standard and a divine one.
questions.quote: The first recorded murder was met by immediate punishment: Cain was exiled for murdering his brother (Genesis 4). In Genesis 9:6, God says, "Whoever sheds man’s blood, by man his blood shall be shed; for in the image of God He made man."

questions.question: How can punishing women who abort actually be loving?
questions.audience: Skeptics who see abolitionism as unloving or harsh
questions.answer: Holland's central move is to redefine love as truth-telling rather than leniency. He argues that exempting mothers from punishment lets them keep believing their abortion was morally fine, and that withholding consequences cooperates with that lie instead of countering it.
questions.quote: It is not loving to enable a person to believe lies. Providing exceptions that allow mothers who commit abortions to go unpunished enables them to believe the lie that their actions were not immoral; therefore, it is not loving to allow those mothers to go unpunished.

questions.question: Does the author think punishment can lead to repentance?
questions.audience: Pastors and discipleship leaders thinking about sin, forgiveness, and consequences
questions.answer: Yes. Holland points to the cycle in the book of Judges, where Israel repents only after suffering the consequences of its sin, and applies the same pattern to abortion. He is careful to add that repentance does not erase the punishment, because that would create a double standard.
questions.quote: Punishment for abortion can lead to repentance. How often in the book of Judges does Israel repent after receiving the natural consequences of their wickedness? This pattern is repeated over and over again throughout the book.

questions.question: How does criminalizing abortion change the broader culture?
questions.audience: Legislators and policy advocates drafting abortion-criminalization bills
questions.answer: Holland argues a strict legal standard sends an unambiguous moral signal: abortion is wrong. He expects some people to reject that message, but believes others who haven't thought carefully will reconsider once the act is treated as a crime.
questions.quote: A strict standard of punishment for those who commit abortions makes the message clear: Abortion is immoral. This can change the way society views abortion.

questions.question: What does the author propose abolitionists should actually do?
questions.audience: Abolitionists building the case for equal protection laws
questions.answer: He calls for criminalizing abortion outright and establishing real punishment for those who commit it, framing this as the loving Christian response rather than a punitive or hateful one. The motivation, he insists, must be love for sinners and a desire to see them repent.
questions.quote: We seek to provide a loving answer to the sin of abortion: criminalize that act and establish proper punishment. That is the most loving thing to do.

related.title: A Mother Is a Magistrate: Why Duress Is No Defense for Abortion
related.reason: Directly takes up the same question of maternal culpability and rejects exemptions; references the Norman Statement Holland signed.

related.title: Fruits of Abolitionism: Is True Repentance Necessary?
related.reason: Same site, same theme of repentance vs. partial pro-life compromise.

related.title: Incremental Laws (of All Sorts) Undermine Repentance
related.reason: Argues, like Holland, that exempting mothers from punishment short-circuits the path to repentance.

related.title: On Repentance, Abolitionists Must Lead by Example: An Open Letter
related.reason: Companion treatment of repentance as central to the abolitionist message.

related.title: Letter to Oklahoma Senators From a Post-Abortive Mother
related.reason: First-person testimony from a post-abortive mother who supports being held accountable, illustrating Holland's argument about consequences leading to repentance.

related.title: Abortion is Murder and Everybody Knows it
related.reason: Foundational claim Holland builds on: that abortion is murder and should be treated as such legally.

related.title: SBC President Bart Barber Gives a Lesson in How Not to Repent
related.reason: Critique of mainstream pro-life leaders who reject equal protection for the same reasons Holland targets.
